questions.question: Which HTML5 element represents a standalone piece of content that could be distributed independently, such as a blog post or news article?
questions.explanation: The <article> element represents a self-contained composition that could be independently distributed or reused, such as a blog post, news article, forum post, or comment.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the <aside> element?
choices: To define the main navigation links
To contain content tangentially related to the surrounding content
To mark the footer of a section
To define the primary content of the page
questions.explanation: The <aside> element represents content that is tangentially related to the content around it. Common uses include sidebars, pull quotes, advertising, and groups of related links.

questions.question: Which element should be used to wrap the dominant content of the <body>, excluding headers, footers, and navigation?
questions.explanation: The <main> element represents the dominant content of the <body>. It should not include content that is repeated across pages like site navigation, headers, or footers. There should be only one <main> element per page.

questions.question: How does the <section> element differ from the <div> element?
choices: There is no difference; they are interchangeable
<section> represents a thematic grouping of content and typically has a heading
<section> can only contain text, while <div> can contain any elements
questions.explanation: The <section> element represents a thematic grouping of content, typically with a heading. Unlike <div>, which is a generic container with no semantic meaning, <section> conveys that its contents are related and form a distinct part of the document.

questions.question: The <___> element defines a set of navigation links for a website.
questions.explanation: The <nav> element is used to define a section of navigation links. It is intended for major navigation blocks, not every group of links on a page.

questions.question: The <header> and <___> elements can appear at the top and bottom of a <section> or <article>.
questions.explanation: The <header> and <footer> elements can be used within <section>, <article>, or the <body> itself. <header> typically contains introductory content or navigation, while <footer> contains information about the section such as authorship, copyright, or related links.
